items.title: Air Canada cuts more flights due to soaring jet fuel prices
items.summary_auto: Air travel continues to be impacted by soaring fuel prices. On Thursday, Air Canada confirmed that, due to the current high cost of jet fuel, it’s halting service earlier than planned on four seasonal flights to U.S. destinations.

items.title: Privacy watchdog finds thousands of tax account breaches, urges stronger protections
items.summary_auto: The federal privacy watchdog says there have been more than 42,000 breaches at the Canada Revenue Agency since 2020 as a result of people gaining unauthorized access to, or modifying, taxpayer information.

items.title: Poilievre tells Conservatives he will stay on and 'keep fighting' after Carney secures majority
items.summary_auto: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre made the case for his continuing leadership to party faithful Thursday, saying he will keep fighting the Liberals even after party turbulence on his watch helped deliver Prime Minister Mark Carney a majority government.

items.title: In meeting with Pope Leo, Rubio tries to minimize Trump's attacks
items.summary_auto: U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio met Pope Leo at the Vatican on Thursday morning, in a visit that was officially about diplomacy but in reality was about cleaning up after President Donald Trump’s latest swipes at the first American pope.

items.title: Elections Alberta says it has issued 568 cease-and-desist letters over Centurion Project leak
items.summary_auto: More than 500 Albertans who accessed a public, searchable database that revealed the personal information of millions of voters have been issued cease-and-desist letters.

items.title: International Olympic Committee supports lifting ban on Belarusian athletes in international sports
items.summary_auto: In a political shift Thursday, the International Olympic Committee said athletes from Belarus should once again compete with their full national identity and not be vetted for neutral status, even as the war in Ukraine continues.

items.title: Fact check: Are wages rising faster than inflation?
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney has said wages are rising faster than inflation. CBC’s Fact Check team dives deeper into that claim

items.title: Wider mix of substances being discovered in Calgary drug supply, agencies see overdose spike
items.summary_auto: In testing over the past three months, University of Calgary researchers have found a wider variety of substances mixed together in the city's illicit drugs, which comes as several front-line agencies are responding to an increase in drug poisonings.

items.title: Procurement delays putting Mounties at risk, says union
items.summary_auto: More than a decade after the intended lifespan of their pistols lapsed, Mounties across Canada are getting a new sidearm — but their union says the significant delay shows why the RCMP's procurement process needs to be overhauled.

items.title: They shared their fears about high-speed rail. Then their comments disappeared
items.summary_auto: A teenager worried about his family's home and an 84-year-old concerned about agricultural impacts both saw the online comments they left on Alto's interactive map taken down. The Crown corporation says their feedback has been restored, but the initial removal has shaken their faith in the consultation process.

items.title: ​Cat dies after catching highly pathogenic avian flu in southeast Sask.
items.summary_auto: A domestic cat in southeastern Saskatchewan has died after contracting "highly pathogenic avian influenza," according to a news release from the provincial Ministry of Agriculture.

items.title: Louise Arbour to be installed Canada's next governor general June 8
items.summary_auto: Louise Arbour will be installed as Canada's next governor general on June 8.

items.title: Vancouver approves 6-month delivery robot pilot program
items.summary_auto: Serve Robotics, a U.S.-based company, will run the pilot program in the downtown and Kitsilano neighbourhoods, starting this fall.

items.title: Manitoba declares public health emergency as HIV rates rise
items.summary_auto: Manitoba has declared a public health emergency as the province continues to see some of the highest HIV rates in Canada, the provincial chief public health officer announced Thursday.

items.title: Montreal among 4 cities to host final Olympic qualifier ahead of Los Angeles Games
items.summary_auto: Montreal is one of four cities that will host final Olympic qualifiers ahead of the 2028 Summer Games in Los Angeles, the International Olympic Committee announced Thursday.

items.title: Stores across Sudbury, Ont., sell out of bottled water as advisory continues
A marmot mystery: southern species pops up in northern B.C.
items.summary_auto: A yellow-bellied marmot has made it's home under a building at the University of Northern British Columbia's Prince George campus. Researchers say this marmot marks the most northerly sighting of the species ever recorded.

items.title: Australian women, children linked to ISIS return from Syria
items.summary_auto: Airliners carrying 13 Australian women and children with alleged ties to the Islamic State group landed in on Thursday in their homeland, where the adults face potential criminal charges relating to their alleged time in the extremist organization's so-called caliphate, which spanned Syria and Iraq.

items.title: N.L. man accused of killing his estranged wife tells trial her disappearance was hard on him
'Very gentle soul': Family, friends devastated after Métis woman, 77, killed in Saskatoon
items.summary_auto: On a day dedicated to honouring missing and murdered Indigenous women and girls, family and friends of Dory Cook had very fresh grief in their hearts.

items.title: Poll finds many Albertans want province to have greater say in who immigrates
items.summary_auto: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith plans to hold a referendum in October and ask several questions about immigration. To get an early sense of how Albertans are feeling, CBC News included two related questions in a poll by Janet Brown Opinion Research last month.

items.title: Some N.S. health-care workers say patient safety at risk as new records system expands
items.summary_auto: Some frontline health-care workers in Nova Scotia's central zone tell CBC News they feel unprepared and ill-trained for the expansion of the province's new medical records system on Saturday, and fear patients could be impacted.

items.title: In the days before she died, no one called for medical help. Her family wants change
items.summary_auto: Johanna Sutcliffe's family is critical of the care she received in her final days, and they’re calling for systemic change to close what they see as a gap in provincial standards of care at special care homes.

items.title: Care workers say company scrambling to replace staff as long-standing workers leave
items.summary_auto: Workers at a private company the Nova Scotia government contracts to care for deeply vulnerable people say Arden Professional Client Care is scrambling to replace some of its most experienced staff.

items.title: The night started with a $25 online sports bet. It ended in a $14K loss
items.summary_auto: Changes to gambling laws in 2021 have given rise to apps that allow users to bet money on sporting events, along with built-in casinos offering games like blackjack, roulette or poker. One study shows young men are particularly at risk of harm.

items.title: The drone war comes home: Canada scrambles to shield military bases in legal grey zone
items.summary_auto: Canada’s military is quietly deploying counter-drone defences at ports and air bases, racing to keep pace with lessons from Ukraine’s devastating strikes on Russian assets. The effort exposes a legal and operational grey zone — how to stop airborne threats over Canadian cities without disrupting civilian life or crossing regulatory lines.

items.title: These students are limiting their own smartphone, social media and tech use. Here's why
items.summary_auto: Some young Canadians are getting more intentional with their technology use and opting for digital minimalism — a paring down of all-encompassing devices like smartphones — of their own volition.

items.title: Canadian government to pay $8.7M to settle data breach class-action involving CRA accounts
items.summary_auto: The federal government will pay $8.7 million to settle a class-action lawsuit involving tens of thousands of Canadians whose sensitive information was compromised or stolen when hackers got into their accounts on government websites, including the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) portal.

items.title: 5 years after Quebec law, 129 Indigenous families still need answers on missing children
items.summary_auto: According to a recent government report, 129 Indigenous families have started the process of searching for 221 missing children since the law, known as Bill 79, came into effect.

items.title: Quebec family mourns after 911 calls for father's heart attack misrouted to Ontario
items.summary_auto: A man in Quebec's Abitibi-Témiscamingue region is questioning if his father would still be alive if his family's 911 calls were properly transferred to Quebec, instead of landing in Ontario. By the time paramedics arrived, roughly 50 minutes had passed from the first 911 call.

items.title: Why disarming Hezbollah is about much more than guns and rockets
items.summary_auto: Facing an existential breaking point, Lebanon’s government is attempting to disarm Hezbollah and assert state sovereignty following a fragile, often-violated April ceasefire with Israel. The path forward remains a dangerous gamble.

items.title: Mexico trade mission launches in Toronto as businesses seek to expand ties with Canada
items.summary_auto: A major Mexican trade mission to Canada launched Thursday, involving over 200 Mexican businesses seeking to expand commercial links during the two-day, two-city encounter that will include high-level talks on the Canada-US-Mexico Agreement.

items.title: Children's aid system faces heightened calls for change after Ontario couple's murder, torture convictions
items.summary_auto: The judge in the trial for an Ontario couple who'd been caring for two brothers was clear in laying blame: Becky Hamber and Brandy Cooney are the ones who committed murder and other crimes. He also noted that the Children's Aid Society (CAS) had "zero to do" with their guilt or innocence. Still, the case raises concerns about the child protection system and has showcased systemic issues that need to be addressed immediately, say advocates and other observers.

items.title: Advocates slam Health Canada's new voluntary breast implant registry, MP 'deeply disappointed'
items.summary_auto: Health Canada has launched a new voluntary registry that will allow patients and health-care professionals to receive direct alerts about breast implant recalls and safety issues, but some patient advocates and medical professionals say it doesn't go far enough.

items.title: 1st wave of free FIFA Fan Festival tickets in Toronto snapped up in 4 hours
items.summary_auto: The first wave of free tickets to the FIFA Fan Festival in Toronto in June were snapped up on Wednesday within four hours of being made available online, an official says.
